skill.name: gtars
skill.description: Use gtars guidance to analyze genomic intervals, BED files, coverage tracks, overlap queries, tokenizers, reference sequences, and fragment workflows with Python or CLI tools.
skill.summary: Analyze genomic intervals and coverage workflows with gtars Python and CLI guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are Markdown examples for legitimate gtars usage. The remaining risk is medium because the skill encourages CLI execution, local file reads and writes, memory-mapped access, and BEDbase fetch/update workflows that can touch user data or the network.

medium_findings.title: CLI workflows can execute local data-processing commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides many gtars command examples that read input files and write indexes, coverage tracks, split outputs, and score files. This is legitimate for a genomics tool, but assistants should ask before running commands that modify files or process sensitive datasets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, but they are documented workflows rather than hidden execution. Risk depends on whether an assistant runs them without user approval or with untrusted paths.

medium_findings.title: Network cache workflow can fetch remote BEDbase data
medium_findings.description: The bbcache documentation includes fetch, list, clear, and update operations for BEDbase.org data. This is expected functionality, but it can contact an external service and alter local cache state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names BEDbase.org and shows fetch and update commands. No exfiltration is present, but network access and cache mutation are clear.

medium_findings.title: Large local files may be memory-mapped for performance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends streaming and memory-mapped access for large BED and genome files. This is a normal performance feature, but it increases local filesystem and resource-use risk for large or sensitive datasets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Memory-mapped mode is clearly documented, but it is not malicious by itself. The concern is operational impact and sensitive local data access.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution detections are mostly Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many code fences and CLI snippets as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples, not embedded scripts or automatic execution hooks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced Markdown examples with gtars commands. No code in the skill automatically executes them.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography detections are false positives in refget context
low_findings.description: The refget documentation describes sequence digests and states that refget-compliant digests use SHA-512 truncated to 48 bytes. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or password hashing guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The digest section explicitly references SHA-512 truncation, which is protocol-specific sequence identification. The surrounding lines discuss validation, not security-sensitive authentication.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI invocation examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs use of gtars CLI commands for local analysis. These commands should be treated as user-approved operations because they can read input files and create or update outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present throughout CLI documentation, but the commands are domain-appropriate and not obfuscated. Risk comes from execution context, not malicious content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem-intensive genomic workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples load, stream, export, split, and memory-map genomic files. This is expected behavior for gtars but should be disclosed because the skill works directly with user datasets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly shows file reads and writes across Python and CLI examples. No hidden file traversal or secret collection was found.

content.value_statement: Genomic interval analysis often needs fast overlap, coverage, reference, and tokenization workflows. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through gtars Python and CLI usage for practical bioinformatics tasks.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use gtars Python APIs versus CLI commands.
Guides overlap detection with IGD indexes and BED region sets.
Shows coverage track workflows for WIG and BigWig outputs.
Supports genomic tokenization workflows for machine learning preprocessing.
Documents refget sequence digest and reference retrieval workflows.
Covers fragment splitting and scoring for single-cell genomics data.
limitations: Requires gtars or gtars-cli to be installed before commands can run.
Does not include real genomic datasets or validate biological conclusions.
Large genome files can require substantial memory, disk, and processing time.
Some examples depend on optional gtars features enabled at installation.

use_cases.title: Compare peak sets
use_cases.description: Find overlaps between ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, promoter, or variant BED files using gtars region operations.

use_cases.title: Build coverage tracks
use_cases.description: Generate WIG or BigWig coverage outputs from fragment or read interval files for browser visualization.

use_cases.title: Prepare genomic ML inputs
use_cases.description: Convert genomic regions into tokenizer outputs for downstream models and geniml-style workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right gtars workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have BED files and need genomic interval analysis. Explain which gtars module to use and what inputs I need.

prompt_templates.title: Run overlap analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare two BED files with gtars overlap or IGD commands. Include expected outputs and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create coverage tracks
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a gtars workflow to generate BigWig coverage tracks from fragment files for genome browser review.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a large genomics pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my gtars pipeline for large BED and FASTA files. Recommend streaming, memory mapping, indexing, and parallel settings.

output_examples.input: User asks how to find peaks that overlap promoters.
output_examples.output: A short workflow that loads both BED files, selects overlap detection, writes an overlap result file, and checks counts.

output_examples.input: User asks how to create a BigWig coverage file from fragments.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step coverage plan with required inputs, output format choice, resolution guidance, and file validation steps.

output_examples.input: User asks how to tokenize regions for genomic machine learning.
output_examples.output: A tokenizer setup plan that describes region inputs, tokenizer construction, batch tokenization, and downstream model handoff.

best_practices: Confirm input file paths, genome build, and coordinate conventions before running analysis.
Use small test files before processing whole-genome datasets or large fragment collections.
Ask before running commands that write outputs, clear caches, or fetch remote data.
anti_patterns: Do not mix genome builds when comparing intervals or reference sequences.
Do not run cache-clearing or overwrite commands without confirming expected outputs.
Do not treat digest matches or overlap counts as biological conclusions without review.

faq.question: What is gtars used for?
faq.answer: gtars supports fast genomic interval work, including overlap queries, coverage generation, tokenization, reference sequence handling, and fragment processing.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users should approve commands before execution, especially when files or caches change.

faq.question: Can it work with BED files?
faq.answer: Yes. BED interval files are a core input for overlap, coverage, tokenizer, and region set workflows.

faq.question: Does it support Python and CLI workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains when to use the Python API and when CLI commands are more suitable.

faq.question: Can it help with machine learning preprocessing?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers genomic tokenizers that convert regions into discrete tokens for downstream models.

faq.question: What security precautions are important?
faq.answer: Review file paths, avoid untrusted inputs, confirm network fetches, and test on small datasets before large runs.
